EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions due to low rainfall have lead to severe to extreme (D2-D3) drought conditions for portions of the Big Island.
A wildfire broke out near the Maui Veterans Highway and closed the road in both directions.  The fire burned about 35 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire led to the closure of Veterans Highway from North Kihei Road to Hansen Road. The fire burned around thirty five acres.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of strong wind gusts cracked a utility pole forcing it to lean over Highway 11 near mile marker 51 near Pahala. The stronger wind gusts also generated damage to trees and utility lines along Wood Valley and South Point roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable conditions settled in over the state which generated a few thunderstorms with some hail across Oahu.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media supplied photos of hail, which fell near Nanakuli, that appeared to be slightly larger than a quarter (between 1 and 1 1/4). Time of occurrence was estimated based on radar imagery. Wahiawa also reported pea sized hail  (1/4) during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surf heights of 10 to 15 feet affected the east facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island.
An extended period of heavy rain, falling at a rate of one to two inches per hour, generated flash flooding that forced road closures for portions of Kauai.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kuhio Highway was closed at the Hanalei Bridge around 8:10am due to water across the road. Later that morning Hauaala Road was closed at the Keapana Bridge due to flooding in Kapaa Stream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall, falling at rates of one to three inches per hour, generated flashing flooding which forced road closures for parts of Big island's Kau and Puna Districts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single vehicle attempted to cross a flooded roadway near Wood Valley Road. The vehicle was carried downstream with operator inside. When emergency personnel arrived, the operator had already self-extricated from the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall, falling at rates of one to three inches per hour, generated flashing flooding which forced road closures for parts of Big island's Kau and Puna Districts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were closed as a result of the repeated rounds of heavy rainfall, including Highway 11 at Kawa Flats, Wood Valley Road, Pohaku Drive, East Kawailani Street, and Kaalaiki Road. Railroad Ave. was also closed in both directions from East Lanikaula St. to Kukil St. Additionally, Piake St. and Llima St. were affected by water flooding the bridge at Paauau Gulch near Pahala.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large and rough conditions affected East facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui and the Big Island.  An extended period of strong easterly trade winds generated surf of 10 to 15 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy data showed warning level surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large and rough conditions affected East facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui and the Big Island.  An extended period of strong easterly trade winds generated surf of 10 to 15 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy data showed warning level surf.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy data showed warning level surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large and rough conditions affected East facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui and the Big Island.  An extended period of strong easterly trade winds generated surf of 10 to 15 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy data showed warning level surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Radar indicated heavy rain across portions of Maui County through the early morning hours. The most intense rain fell over the Wailuku and Waikapu areas with rainfall rates of one to two inches per hour over saturated ground. The Maalaea area of south Maui and the Honoapiilani Highway from Maalaea to Lahaina were the most vulnerable to flooding impacts, as well poor drainage areas in Wailuku and Kahului. The Honopou Stream gage on the windward Haleakala slopes also showed rapid increase in water levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Makawao Avenue was closed at Maliko Gulch between Kee Road and Kokomo Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of persistent heavy rainfall moved over Kauai from the southeast at rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour.  Water levels rose on streams across much of the island, especially in the South Fork Wailua and Hanalei Rivers. Runoff impacted vulnerable low lying areas and roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maalo Road was closed beginning at 2:00pm near Wailua Falls Lookout due to flash flooding. Additionally, flash flooding also forced the closure of the cement bridge near Kawaihau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall elevated streams across the island with efficient runoff due to high soil moisture content and locally intense rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kuhio Highway near the Hanalei Bridge was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable air generated some thunderstorms across the Hawaiian Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report that a lightning strike in the Ewa Beach area split a tree. No other damage was reported from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty east winds sustained up to 35 kts with localized gusts near 50 kts generated some damage for portions of the Hawaiian Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that strong winds had blown a large tree across Kohala Mountain Road near Kahua Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty east winds sustained up to 35 kts with localized gusts near 50 kts generated some damage for portions of the Hawaiian Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported that Pensacola St. was closed between Nehoa St. to Piikoi St. due to a downed pole and electrical wires.  It was also reported that more than 2,100 customers were without power in the Makiki area due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty east winds sustained up to 35 kts with localized gusts near 50 kts generated some damage for portions of the Hawaiian Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was reported that the Kalakaua Ave. east bound lane was closed near Diamond Head water fountain due to a fallen tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty east winds sustained up to 35 kts with localized gusts near 50 kts generated some damage for portions of the Hawaiian Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported that a tree had fallen onto Komo Mai Dr. at Aumakua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty east winds sustained up to 35 kts with localized gusts near 50 kts generated some damage for portions of the Hawaiian Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported that Kokea St. was closed between King St. and Dillingham Blvd. due to multiple downed utility poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty east winds sustained up to 35 kts with localized gusts near 50 kts generated some damage for portions of the Hawaiian Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported that Likelike Hwy, Kaneohe bound, was closed due to fallen tree which fell on the Kalihi side before the tunnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty east winds sustained up to 35 kts with localized gusts near 50 kts generated some damage for portions of the Hawaiian Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park staff reported that an approximately 16-inch diameter eucalyptus tree fell across Haleakala Hwy, State Route 377, at mile 3 of State Highway 377.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty east winds sustained up to 35 kts with localized gusts near 50 kts generated some damage for portions of the Hawaiian Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee walking campus to work noted several large tree limbs down on the University of Hawaii Campus at Manoa, near the Hamilton library.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of gusty east winds sustained up to 35 kts with localized gusts near 50 kts generated some damage for portions of the Hawaiian Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported that one lane of the Pali Highway was closed due to a fallen tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed on the Big Island and generated several lightning strikes. The strikes hit in a location that is typically the island's dry side.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media initially reported that lightning strikes sparked two brush fires near  the Keamuku Maneuver Area in the Pohakuloa Training Area. Based on hot-spot satellite imagery taken from FIRMS (Fire Information for Resource Management System), the fires were extinguished Feb 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of deep layered moisture was anchored over the Big Island through the period, with temperatures at summit level remaining near or below freezing. This pattern generated a mix of heavy snow and freezing rain, which produced up to 12 inches of snowfall and significant icing. Travel to the summits was considered extremely dangerous and life threatening due to snow and ice covered roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park Rangers relayed to the National Weather Service that summit roads were closed to the public due to occasional whiteout and continued deteriorating weather conditions. Crews tried to clear the roads at times during the event, though snow continued to fall. The roads were at times open to observatory personnel, however those personnel were encouraged to use studded tires or chains when attempting to drive up to the summits.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy data showed warning level surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of enhanced rainfall with embedded locally heavy rainfall rates overwhelmed a few local roads on Pearl City Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Officials reported that the Pearl City Peninsula Compound main gate and Victor Wharf Access Road were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Soils on the windward side of the Big Island, near Hilo, were nearly saturated and fatigued due previous rounds of heavy rainfall that occurred over the past several days, which created a landslide near Honomu.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 19 near mile marker 13 was closed in both directions due to a landslide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Radar and automated rain gauges indicated heavy rain, with rates of one to two inches per hour, had fallen over northern Kauai. Area river and stream gauges showed elevated flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kuhio Highway was closed near the Hanalei Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of deep moisture and instability over the Big Island generated a mix of freezing rain, heavy snow and strong winds across the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa (above 12,500 feet).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Summit roads were closed for a period during this stretch due to high winds and snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of deep layered moisture and moderate instability generated periods of heavy rainfall and localized flooding impacts across portions of Maui County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kamehameha V Highway near Kaunakakai was closed due to flash flooding. There were rapid rises on the Kawela Gulch and the Kaunakakai Gulch stream gauges and homes were flooded in the Kawela Gulch area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extra-large northwest swell (320-340 degrees) pushed across the island chain during the event.  This generated surf of 25 to 35 feet along north and west facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, north facing shores of Maui. Surf of 12 to 16 feet was noted along west facing shores of Big Island.

An extra large northwest (310-320 degree) swell produced extremely dangerous giant sized surf. Coastal water overwash also occurred across many low lying roadways along northern and western facing shorelines, especially during periods of high tide.  A high surf|warning was issued for most north and west facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui and the west facing shores of Big Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hookipa Beach Park was closed due to high surf, as waves were overtopping a break wall into the parking lot and other areas beyond the break wall. Baldwin beach was closed due to high surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A kona low developed west-northwest of the Hawaiian Islands and brought periods of heavy rainfall and isolated thunderstorms. Heavy rainfall lead to localized flash flooding and landslides across portions of the State. Impacts from the kona low continued into December 2023 and are covered in episode 186869.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hawaii Police Department reported a landslide closing the Hawaii Belt Rd (Highway 19) between Mile Marker Twelve and Honomu Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extra large northwest (310-320 degree) swell produced extremely dangerous giant sized surf. Coastal water overwash also occurred across many low lying roadways along northern and western facing shorelines, especially during periods of high tide.  A high surf|warning was issued for most north and west facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui and the west facing shores of Big Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the injuries (four injuries were due to people being washed off the rocks at Sharks Cove and two injuries were reported at a surf event), Ocean Safety officials rescued 21 people and took more than 3,000 preventative actions related to the dangerous surf. Sand and debris was washed across Kamehameha Highway at Rockpiles and Laniakea Beach, plus along a portion of the Ke Nui bike path. Waves also inundated the parking lot at Sharks Cover and overtopped a break wall that protects homes and the public access road to Keiki Beach.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide was reported near Mile Marker 28 in Kawaili Gulch along Hawaii Belt rd. (Highway 19).

Surf of 12 to 16 feet was noted along west facing shores of Big Island.

A large northwest swell brought thirty five to fifty foot surf for north and west facing shores. These large breaking waves and strong current made recreational and maritime activities dangerous, as well as produced localized coastal flooding, property damage, and debris from the beach runup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surf up to 50 feet brought hazardous conditions to north and west facing shores. Members of the public reported, property damage near rockpiles took out a fence. The, blue wave house at pipeline had its ground floor completely washed out. glass doors blown out. Furniture and carpet throughout has to be discarded. Large breaking waves and current also made recreational and maritime activities dangerous.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kamehameha Highway was closed in both directions at Kawailoa Drive due to a downed tree on power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rainfall lead to an accumulation of water on the roadways which led to the closure of Piilani highway from mile marker 29-31, as well as mile marker 16. Hana Highway was also closed near mile marker 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy rain falling at a rate of one to three inches per hour initiated river rises on the Hanalei River in northern Kauai.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kuhio Highway was closed at the Hanalei River Bridge due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Observing equipment reported gusts as high as 39-48 kts which uprooted some trees, knocked out power, and displaced shingles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a fallen tree that closed the Pali Highway in the Honolulu bound direction from Kamehameha Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Observing equipment reported gusts as high as 39-48 kts which uprooted some trees, knocked out power, and displaced shingles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The utility company reported 24,000 customers without power on the island of Oahu due to strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Observing equipment reported gusts as high as 39-48 kts which uprooted some trees, knocked out power, and displaced shingles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that shingles were blown off and minor tree damage occurred due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Observing equipment reported gusts as high as 39-48 kts which uprooted some trees, knocked out power, and displaced shingles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported an uprooted tree in their yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moved through the area and produced a wintry mix of light snow and  freezing rain over the Big Island Summits. Winds were gusty at times with wind gusts as high as 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix with 1 to 3 inches of snow fell along with freezing rain creating hazardous conditions across the Summits. There were also wind gusts as high as 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and wind combined to generate some hazards on the Big Island Summits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of snow affected the Summits with the heaviest snows above 12000 feet while a mix of rain and snow fell as low as 10500 feet. Total snow accumulations were up to 5 inches. Southeast winds gusted up to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was sufficient to flood and close roads in urban Honolulu.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oahu emergency management reported the School Street onramp to H1 Freeway was|closed due to water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was sufficient to flood and close roads in urban Honolulu.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that the roads near Acacia Rd and Kuala St. along  |Kamehameha Highway was flooded in Pearl City. 6-12 inches of water was reported on the roadway with vehicles slow to move through.  Also, one lane was closed on H1  and Punahou St, due  to flooding over the road. Additionally, cars were stranded in ponding water on the off ramp at H1 and Vineyard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain, occasionally mixed with light snow, caused hazardous conditions across the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations were around one inch and ice accretion was around one tenth of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall was able to close some roads across the eastern half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flooding on Highway 11 near Kawa Flats between mile marker 50.5 and mile marker 60. The road was closed due to water over the road between Pahala and Naalehu.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall was able to close some roads across the eastern half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Piilani Highway was closed between mile markers 29 and 31 due to unpassable flooding waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding impacts due to locally heavy rainfall were reported across portions of Oahu.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood water flowed over Kamehameha Highway and Waikane Valley Road near Waikane Stream. Police closed Kamehameha Hwy in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy rainfall led to road closures on the Big Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters were reported on Highway 19 between mile marker 70 and 71. Both lanes were closed due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A kona low developed west-northwest of the Hawaiian Islands and brought periods of heavy rainfall and isolated thunderstorms. Heavy rainfall lead to localized flash flooding and landslides across portions of the State. Impacts from the kona low continued into December 2023 and are covered in episode 186869.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rainfall lead to water accumulating on the roads which prompted the road closure of Wood Valley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf caused by a large south swell created hazardous conditions for the south facing shores such as strong currents, large breaking waves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Kilauea volcano erupted and caused minor to moderate gas and haze, or vog around portions of the Big Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The volcanic eruption of Kilauea released gasses into the air which decreased the air quality as well as the visibility. On August 8, several wildfires ignited over Maui and the Big Island. Very strong trade winds, low relative humidity, and ongoing drought conditions across leeward sections led to rapid fire growth and spread. The fires prompted several mandatory evacuations and emergency proclamations. One wildfire destroyed the town of Lahaina, Maui, killing 102 people and injuring at least 32.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brushfire erupted in Kula, burned 200 acres and prompted evacuations. Nineteen homes were reportedly destroyed by the wildfire. There were no known injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 8, several wildfires ignited over Maui and the Big Island. Very strong trade winds, low relative humidity, and ongoing drought conditions across leeward sections led to rapid fire growth and spread. The fires prompted several mandatory evacuations and emergency proclamations. One wildfire destroyed the town of Lahaina, Maui, killing 102 people and injuring at least 32.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brushfire near Waimea burned 1,000 acres and threatened the Kohala Range subdivision. Firefighters from several agencies battled the blaze and kept it from destroying any homes. A few homes were damaged but no cost estimate was available. There were no reports of injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 8, several wildfires ignited over Maui and the Big Island. Very strong trade winds, low relative humidity, and ongoing drought conditions across leeward sections led to rapid fire growth and spread. The fires prompted several mandatory evacuations and emergency proclamations. One wildfire destroyed the town of Lahaina, Maui, killing 102 people and injuring at least 32.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire near Lahaina was reported at 6:40 am HST and was contained within 3 hours. Another fire was reported around 3:30pm and prompted evacuation orders by 4:30 pm. However, power lines downed by the damaging winds closed roads and restricted evacuation routes. Wildfire continued to spread rapidly and quickly became out of control. The Coast Guard rescued people entering the Pacific Ocean who were escaping the fire. They rescued seventeen people including two children and assisted forty survivors on shore. At around 11:52 pm the Hawaii National guard was deployed on Maui as wildfires continued to burn out of control.  At 1:20 AM on August 9, over 14,000 customers were without power, and at 5:03 AM, the fire damaged much of the historic banyan tree that was imported to Maui in 1873 from India. At 9:25 am on August 9, over 2,100 people were seeking refuge in Maui shelters, a number that would increase throughout the month, reaching a peak of 8,000 in September. At 12:45 pm on August 9, more than 14,000 people were transported off Maui with another 14,500 waiting to be moved. The fire burned 2,170 acres and destroyed most of the historic town of Lahaina, including over 2,200 structures burned mostly or completely to the ground. There were 102 confirmed fatalities and at least 32 injuries. The fire also destroyed about 4,000 cars, of which 1,000 had been abandoned on public roadways. As of late October, the Coast Guard had recovered and removed 86 vessels from Lahaina Small Boat Harbor, in addition to 9,400 gallons of petroleum products and 3,300 pounds of hazardous materials.
